coredata.dc:title: Constructing nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots embedded in CNT supported layered (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8 self-supporting film for high-performance supercapacitor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Rong
Lin, Huachen
Sun, Lin
Ying, Yulong
He, Bin
Liu, Yu
coredata.dc:description: To improve the electrochemical performance of positive electrode materials, constructing graded nanostructures is a worthwhile approach. This study successfully synthesized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD) modified (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8 on a carbon nanotube (CNT) substrate to construct self-supporting electrodes for high-performance supercapacitors. The (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8 nanosheets were successfully wrapped onto the CNT surface through a solution impregnation process, which increased the specific surface area and interlayer spacing of the material. Furthermore, the electrochemical properties of the electrode material underwent significant enhancement due to the synergistic interplay between metal ions and the numerous redox centers. The embedding of the NGQD enriched the materials with active sites and further improved its specific capacity without compromising the structure intergrity of the layer configuration. Using CNT as the substrate ensured the self-supporting nature of the electrode. Consequently, the (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8/NGQD@CNT composite exhibits an ultra-high specific capacitance of 3018.2 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and 2332 F g−1 at 10 A g−1. The asymmetric supercapacitor constructed with (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8/NGQD@CNT and activated carbon (AC) presented an impressive energy density of 160.2 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 800 W kg−1. After 8000 charge–discharge cycles, the capacity retention rate was 78.5 %, with a Coulo mbic efficiency consistently above 98 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supercapacitor
Carbon nanotubes
Transition metal oxides
Energy density
Self-supporting electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrating the synthesis of (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT composite thin films.
SEM images of (a-c) (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT at different magnifications. (d-g) TEM and HRTEM images of (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT. (h-n) elemental distribution mapping of (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT.
XPS spectra for (a) Ni 2p, (b) Co 2p, (c) V 2p, (d) C 1s, (e) O 1s and (f) N 1s of (NC)VO @CNT and (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT composites film.

(a) CV curves of NiCo-LDH@CNT, (NC)VO@CNT and (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT at a scan rate of 2 mV s−1, (b) GCD curves of NiCo-LDH@CNT, (NC)VO@CNT and (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT at a current density of 1 A g−1, (c) CV curves of (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT at different scan rates (2–100 mV s−1), (d) GCD curves of (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT at different current density (1–10 A g−1), (e) Capacity comparison diagram of NiCo-LDH@CNT, (NC)VO@CNT and (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT, (f) EIS curves of NiCo-LDH@CNT, (NC)VO@CNT and (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT.

(a) Schematic diagram of energy storage mechanism of (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT, (b) CV curves of (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT at different scan rates (2–5 mV s−1), (c) the plot of log I p versus log v for the (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT electrode at different scan rates, (d) separation of the capacitive and diffusion currents in the (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT electrode at a scan rate of 2, 3, 4 and 5 mV s−1, respectively, (e) the capacitance ratio of the surface capacitance effect at different scan rates.

(a) The schematic representation of HSC (Hybrid Supercapacitor) construction, (b) CV curves of (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT and AC in a three-electrode system, (c) CV curves of (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT//AC at varying voltage windows, (d) CV curves of (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT//AC at different scan rates ranging from 2 to 100 mV s−1, (e) GCD curves of (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT//AC at various current densities from 1 to 10 A g−1, (f) specific capacitance of (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT//AC.

(a) Ragone plot of the asymmetric (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT//AC device and other supercapacitors, (b) photographs of 4 blue and 4 white bulbs illuminated by two ASC devices, (c) cycling stability of the ASC device, (d) SEM of (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT after cycling stability. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Constructing nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots embedded in CNT supported layered (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8 self-supporting film for high-performance supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Huzhou Key Laboratory of Environmental Functional Materials and Pollution Control, Department of Materials Chemistry, Huzhou University, Huzhou 313000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huzhou Key Laboratory of Environmental Functional Materials and Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Huzhou Key Laboratory of Environmental Functional Materials and Pollution Control, Department of Materials Chemistry, Huzhou University, Huzhou 313000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A self-supporting film embedded with nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots into (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8 nanosheets has been successfully prepared for high-performance supercapacitors.

To improve the electrochemical performance of positive electrode materials, constructing graded nanostructures is a worthwhile approach. This study successfully synthesized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD) modified (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8 on a carbon nanotube (CNT) substrate to construct self-supporting electrodes for high-performance supercapacitors. The (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8 nanosheets were successfully wrapped onto the CNT surface through a solution impregnation process, which increased the specific surface area and interlayer spacing of the material. Furthermore, the electrochemical properties of the electrode material underwent significant enhancement due to the synergistic interplay between metal ions and the numerous redox centers. The embedding of the NGQD enriched the materials with active sites and further improved its specific capacity without compromising the structure intergrity of the layer configuration. Using CNT as the substrate ensured the self-supporting nature of the electrode. Consequently, the (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8/NGQD@CNT composite exhibits an ultra-high specific capacitance of 3018.2 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and 2332 F g−1 at 10 A g−1. The asymmetric supercapacitor constructed with (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8/NGQD@CNT and activated carbon (AC) presented an impressive energy density of 160.2 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 800 W kg−1. After 8000 charge–discharge cycles, the capacity retention rate was 78.5 %, with a Coulo mbic efficiency consistently above 98 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Achieving the global development goals of carbon peaking and carbon neutrality requires advancements in electrochemical energy storage technologies to replace traditional fossil fuels associated with new energy source. Among them, supercapacitors have garnered immense attention as a highly promising solution for energy storage [1,2]. However, one of the primary challenges lies in enhancing the energy density, which is inherently tied to the electrode material's energy storage capacity [3]. Therefore, the rational design and precise preparation of advanced electrode materials are pivotal in enhancing energy density.

Carbon-based materials, including carbon nanotubes (CNT), carbon cloth (CC), and graphene have undergone extensive research as substrates for self-supporting electrodes [4–6]. One-dimensional (1D) CNT are notable for their high conductivity and large specific surface area. Their intertwined network structure provides rich pathways for efficient electron and ion transport [7–9]. Integrating CNT with metal oxides and hydroxides can greatly enhances the conductivity of electrode materials. For instance, Chen et al. prepared a self-supporting CuCo-LDH/BPQD@CNT thin film electrode with a layered porous structure by integrating CNT with Co-MOF, followed by copper ion exchange and BPQD attachment. This structure facilitated a high specific capacity of 1061.6 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 [10].

In recent years, transition metal oxides (TMOs) have been widely explored due to their diverse oxidation states and excellent conductivity, making them suitable for energy storage applications. TMOs contribute to energy storage not only through electrostatic charge accumulation but also via Faraday redox reactions, thereby contributing to high specific capacitance and improved energy density in supercapacitors [11–13]. However, during the charging and discharging cycles, ion insertion/deintercalation can cause volume expansion in TMOs, leading to partial irreversible capacitance attenuation [14,15]. Multi-metallic oxides, with their abundant active centers and high conductivity, and the synergistic effect of multiple metals, can effectively address these challenges [16,17]. Among them, nickel-based and cobalt-based vanadates have been particularly attractive for supercapacitors due to their significant charge storage capacity, high specific capacitance, and exceptional cycling stability. Among these materials, the low oxidation state of nickel and cobalt elements dominates the pseudocapacitive process, while vanadium plays a lesser role. These metal vanadates have abundant oxidation states, especially when combined with CNT, greatly improving the electrochemical performance of supercapacitors [18–20]. Graphene quantum dots (GQD), as emerging carbon nanomaterials, have the advantages of low toxicity and strong electron-donating abilities, positioning them as potential candidates for clean energy applications [21–23]. Nitrogen-doped GQD (NGQD) further improve their electronic properties by providing more active sites, increased specific surface area, and abundant functional groups [24,25]. The combination of NGQDs with (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8 can provide abundant binding sites for the electrochemical reactions. Xu et al. demonstrated this by embedding NGQD onto CuCo-LDH, forming a CC@CuCo-LDH/NGQD electrode with a hierarchical hollow structure. By incorporating NGQD, the conductivity of the electrode material was enhanced, and its active sites were enriched, resulting in a capacitance of 1009.9 F g−1 [26].

In this work, (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8/NGQD@CNT electrode materials with hierarchical structures using CNT as the substrate were prepared by solution impregnation. The morphology and structure composition of (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8/NGQD@CNT composites were determined through a series of characterizations, and their electrochemical performance was evaluated through a series of electrochemical tests such as cyclic voltammetry and constant current charge–discharge tests. The integration of NGQD increased the specific capacitance of (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8@CNT from 2690.7 F g−1 to 3018.2 F g−1, thanks to its high conductivity and abundant surface functional groups of NGQD that provide more redox active centers for electrochemical reactions. The as-assembled (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8/NGQD@CNT//AC asymmetric supercapacitor presented an impressive energy density of 160.2 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 800 W kg−1, and demonstrated robust stability, retaining 78.5 % of its capacity after 8000 cycles. Therefore, the self-supporting thin film electrode based on (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8/NGQD@CNT shows significant potential for applications in high-performance supercapacitors.

NiSO4·6H2O, K2S2O8, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, and NH4VO3 are purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Carbon nanotubes (CNT) are Nanjing/Jiangsu XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

Firstly, 2.5 g of NiSO4·6H2O and 0.5 g K2S2O8 were dissolved in 25 ml of deionized water. Then add 1.25 mL of NH3·H2O to the mixture. Stir the solution for 0.5 h to ensure complete dissolution and obtain a homogeneous Solution A. Dissolve 0.9 g of Co(NO3)·6H2O in 20 mL of deionized water, and then add 1.5 ml of CNT dispersion to the cobalt nitrate solution, forming solution B. Slowly pour solution B to solution A with continuous stirring for 0.5 h. Finally, NiCo-LDH@CNT film were obtained through filtration process, washed twice with deionized water and vacuum dried at 60 °C for 24 h.

Weigh 1 g of triammonium citrate and add 20 ml of deionized water. Reflux in an oil bath at 200 ℃ for 30 min. After the solution turns light orange, add NaOH (10 mg mL−1) to adjust the pH of the solution to neutral, indicating successful preparation of NGQD. The concentration of NGQD solution used in the experiment was 0.005 mg mL−1 (1 mL of NGQD prepared, 9 mL of deionized water) [26,27].

At first, 0.8 mmol of NH4VO3 was dissolved in 25 ml of deionized water, followed by the addition of 1.25 ml of NH3·H2O. The previously prepared film was then immersed in this solution and heated in an oil bath at 80 ℃ for 0.5 h. Once cooled to room temperature, the film was thoroughly rinsed with deionized water to yield (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8@CNT ((NC)VO@CNT). Finally, the (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8@CNT film was immersed in the NGQD solution (10 mL) for 0.5 h to obtain the (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8/NGQD@CNT ((NC)VO/NGQD@CNT) composite. The film obtained above were washed once with deionized water and vacuum dried at 60 °C for 24 h.

Fig. 1 shows the specific synthesis route of the (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8/NGQD@CNT composite. The process begins by preparing two separate solutions: one containing NiSO4·6H2O and K2S2O8, and the other containing Co(NO3)2·6H2O and CNT. These solutions are then mixed together and filtered to obtain NiCo-LDH nanosheets, which grow uniformly on the CNT substrate. This method results in a lightweight and flexible composite without the need of adhesives. Then, the composite undergoes vanadization treatment in an oil bath at 80 ℃. During this step, the NiCo-LDH is converted into (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8, as confirmed by the results. Finally, the (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8 film is immersed in NGQD solution to add more active sites to the sample, enhancing its overall electrochemical performance.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were used to characterize the surface and internal structure of (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT composite, investigating the influence of morphology on the electrochemical performance of electrode materials. Fig. S1 shows the SEM images of CNT (Fig. S1a-b) and NiCo-LDH@CNT (Fig. S1c-d). It can be seen that CNT interweave into a network structure, providing a structural basis for film formation. NiCo-LDH nanosheets are uniformly wrapped around the outside of CNT, but the size of the nanosheets is smaller than that of the final sample, so further modification is needed. Fig. 2 a-c shows the SEM image of (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT. The images reveal that the vanadate nanosheets grow uniformly along the longitudinal axis of the CNT without any observable aggregation. This uniform growth of CNT greatly increases their specific surface area, thereby effectively enhancing the contact area between the electrode material and the electrolyte, which is crucial for improving electrochemical performance. The TEM image in Fig. 2d shows that a network of ultra-thin nanosheets is interlaced and anchored on the CNT. Even after mechanical stress or fracture, these nanosheets remain well-adhered to the CNT, corroborating the SEM image above. Fig. 2e shows the HRTEM image, it can be seen more clearly that Co3V2O8 and Ni3V2O8 nanosheets have grown on CNT. The lattice spacing measured in Fig. 2f, at 0.21 nm, aligns with the (100) crystal plane of NGQD. Furthermore, the lattice spacing of 0.25 nm and 0.15 nm corresponds to the (221) crystal plane of Co3V2O8 and the (200) crystal plane of Ni3V2O8, respectively. These lattice spacings suggest a similar crystalline structure within the electrode material, which contributes to its overall structural integrity and electrochemical functionality.

Fig. S2a shows the XRD pattern of NiCo-LDH@CNT and (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT. According to the standard card PDF#33–0429, the composition of the prepared sample material can be determined to be NiCo-LDH, and at positions 11.57°, 23.34°, and 34.92°correspond to the (003), (006), and (012) crystal planes, respectively. The diffraction peaks positioned at 21.36°, 29.63°, 35.28° and 61.45° belong to the (002), (131), (221) and (262) planes of Co3V2O8 (PDF#70–1393). Similarly, main diffraction peaks at 21.55°, 29.99° and 64.04° belongs to the (200), (131), (442) planes of Ni3V2O8 (PDF#74–1485). By comparing the XRD results above, it was found that after vanadium oxidation treatment, NiCo-LDH will transform into Ni3V2O8 and Co3V2O8. However, due to the suboptimal crystallinity of the sample, some diffraction peaks do not match perfectly with the standard card. To further determine the chemical composition and valence state in (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted. Fig. S2b displays the XPS survey spectra of (NC)VO@CNT and (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT. The spectra confirm the existence of Ni, Co, V, C, O, and N elements in both samples. Notably, a N peak is present even before adding NGQD, plausibly stemming from the inclusion of nitrate ions during the synthesis process. However, the N peak is significantly enhanced after the addition of NGQD, indicating their successful incorporation of NGQD into the composite. The XPS spectra for the Ni element are shown in Fig. 3 a. The curves for both (NC)VO@CNT and (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT are analysed. Compared with (NC)VO@CNT, the addition of NGQD resulted in a positive shift of 0.4 eV in the peaks of Ni2+ on both 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 orbitals. Similarly, for Ni3+, a positive shift of 0.8 eV was observed on the 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 orbitals. This transformation indicates the presence of electron transfer between Ni2+/Ni3+ and NGQD, thereby enhancing the electrochemical reactivity of the electrode material [28,29]. Fig. 3b depicts the XPS spectrum of Co in (NC)VO@CNT and (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT. The peaks observed at 797.7 and 782.5 eV correspond to the 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 orbitals of Co2+ in (NC)VO@CNT, respectively. By comparing the XPS results of the two steps, it can be found that after embedding NGQD, the Co2+ peaks in (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT shift slightly to 798.1 eV (2p1/2) and 783.1 eV (2p3/2). In addition, the Co3+ peaks also exhibit a minor shift after NGQD embedding (from 796.6 eV to 796.7 eV for 2p1/2 and from 781.4 eV to 781.7 eV for 2p3/2), indicating that NGQD affect electron transfer within Co ions [30–32]. From the XPS spectrum of element V in Fig. 3c, the peaks at 515.4, 517.0, and 517.7 eV respectively represent the V3+, V4+, and V5+ states in (NC)VO@CNT, while the characteristic peaks of V3+, V4+, and V5+ in (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT are located at 517.1, 517.9, and 518.7 eV [33,34]. The addition of NGQD caused these peaks to shift towards higher binding energies, resulting in a significant change in peak area, indicating a significant interaction between the vanadium state and NGQD. For element C (Fig. 3d), by comparing the XPS spectra of (NC)VO@CNT and (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT samples, it was found that the embedding of NGQD does not substantially change the electronic environment of carbon. This observation indicates that the intrinsic properties of carbon networks are largely unaffected by the incorporation of NGQDs [35,36]. From the XPS of element O in Fig. 3e, it can be seen that the peak at 531.43 eV corresponds to the M−O bond. By comparing the two samples before and after the addition of NGQD, the changes in peak area and their transition towards higher binding energies indicate that NGQD affects the redox interactions between metal ions [37]. The N 1s spectrum of (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT is shown in Fig. 3f, with peaks located at 398.9, 400.3, and 401.6 eV corresponding to pyridine N, pyrrole N, and graphite N, respectively. Studies suggest that due to their higher electronegativity compared to carbon, N atoms in pyridine N and pyrrole N forms act as electron donors, providing abundant active sites for reactions. Conversely, graphite N acts as an electron acceptor, improving the material’s conductivity [38,39].

To evaluate the electrochemical proficiency of the synthesized electrode materials, a three-electrode system test was conducted. Fig. 4 a presents the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves at a scan rate of 2 mV s−1 for NiCo-LDH@CNT, (NC)VO@CNT, and (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT. As evident from the figure, the (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT electrode exhibits the most extensive CV integral area, accompanied by a distinct redox peak and a significantly higher peak current. This underscores the impact of NGQD incorporation, which enhances the redox active sites within the electrode material, resulting in enhanced conductivity and superior electrochemical performance. Fig. 4b compares the galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) curves at a current density of 1 A g−1 for NiCo-LDH@CNT, (NC)VO@CNT, and (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT. Aligning with the findings from the CV curve analysis, (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT electrode demonstrates the longest charge and discharge plateau, indicative of significant redox activity. Fig. 4c and Fig. S3a-b show the CV curves of NiCo-LDH@CNT, (NC)VO@CNT, and (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT electrodes, respectively, at varying scan rates ranging from 2 to 100 mV s−1, with increments of 5, 10, 20, and 50 mV s−1. As the scan rate increases, the peak current also increases accordingly. The reduction peak in the CV curves shifts towards a lower potential, while the oxidation peak migrates towards a higher potential. This behavior is likely attributed to the polarization effect within the electrode material. The following possible redox reactions occur during this process: [13,20,40].
(1) 3Ni(NO3)2 + H2O+2NH4VO3 ↔ Ni3V2O8 + 2NH3·H2O+6NO2 + 3/2O2
(2) 3Co(NO3)2 + H2O+2NH4VO3 ↔ Co3V2O8 + 2NH3·H2O+6NO2 + 3/2O2
(3) Ni3V2O8 + 6OH− ↔ 3NiOOH+H3V2O8 3− + 3e−
(4) Co3V2O8 + 6OH− ↔ 3CoOOH+H3V2O8 3− + 3e−
(5) NiOOH+OH− ↔ NiO2 + H2O+e−
(6) CoOOH+OH− ↔ CoO2 + H2O+e−

The energy storage mechanism of (NC)VO/NGQD@CNT is shown in Fig. 5 a, and its remarkable electrochemical performance can primarily be attributed to the following key aspects. Firstly, the network structure. Using CNT as the substrate material for self-supporting electrodes creates a network structure that facilitates rich channels for ions and electrons transport within the composite material. This structural arrangement enhances the overall conductivity and ion mobility. Secondly, increased surface area. The abundant nanosheets loaded onto CNT greatly increase the specific surface area of electrode materials. This expanded surface area improves the effective contact between the composite materials and electrolytes, thereby accelerating ion migration and enhancing the overall electrochemical performance. Thirdly, addition of NGQD. The inclusion of NGQD, which possess numerous oxygen-containing functional groups, introduces additional redox-active sites. These sites contribute significantly to the improvement of electrochemical properties by facilitating redox reactions and enhancing the specific capacity of the electrode materials. To further delve into the energy storage mechanism of the electrodes and gain a deeper understanding of the changes in their energy storage process, it is imperative to analyze the pseudocapacitive contributions. By analyzing the pseudocapacitive contribution through the CV curve at a low scan rate (2–5 mV s−1), as shown in Fig. 5b, the corresponding redox peak is marked. The energy storage behavior of the electrode is dictated by the correlation between scan rate (ν) and peak current (i), given by the formula (7) [41]:
(7)
i = a
ν
b
wherein, i is the current (mA), ν denotes the scan rate (mV s−1), and a and b are constants. When b is less than 0.5, the energy storage is mainly diffusion-controlled. Conversely, if b value exceeds 1, the behavior is capacitive. For b value between 0.5–1, a hybrid of both diffusion and capacitive characteristics exists. The linear regression fitted curve shown in Fig. 5c indicates that the b values corresponding to the redox peaks are 0.8076, 0.8836 and 0.8579, respectively, suggesting that capacitive behavior dominates in this material. To accurately quantify the proportion of pseudocapacitive and diffusion-controlled mechanisms to the overall energy storage, the following formula is used (8) [41]:
(8)
i =
k 1
ν +
k 2
ν
0.5
where k1ν represents the pseudocapacitance contribution and k2ν0.5 represents the diffusion-controlled contribution. The results of these calculations are shown in the CV comparison chart in Fig. 5d and the bar chart in Fig. 5e. The specific pseudocapacitive contribution rates are 61.22 %, 63.31 %, 65.13 %, 66.88 %, 68.56 %, 69.95 %, and 71.32 %, respectively, at increasing scan rates. As shown in the figures, the pseudocapacitance contribution increase with higher scanning rate, demonstrating that pseudocapacitance behavior becomes more dominant, consistent with the previous observations.

As shown in Fig. 6 a, (NC)VO/NQGD@CNT was utilized as the positive electrode material, whereas activated carbon (AC) functioned as the negative electrode material, together forming an asymmetric supercapacitor configuration. This configuration was used to evaluate the practical application potential of the electrode materials. Fig. 6b presents a comparative analysis of the CV curves between the positive and negative electrodes. Fig. S5 provides a detailed examination of the electrochemical performance of the activated carbon electrode, revealing a specific capacity of 546.6 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. Using the specific capacity (F g−1) of the positive (C+) and negative (C-) electrodes, the voltage window (V), and the mass of the positive electrode material (1 mg), the mass of the anode material (4.3 mg) can be accurately determined through the application of the provided formula (9):
(9)
m +
m -
=
C -
Δ V
-
C +
Δ V
+

To explore the optimal operating voltage for the device, an analysis of CV curves was conducted across varying voltage windows. As shown in Fig. 6c, slight polarization occurred in the CV curve when the voltage extended to 1.7 V. Therefore, a voltage window of 0–1.6 V was selected as the optimal range for further testing. Two electrode testing was conducted on the device within the optimal voltage window. As depicted in Fig. 6d, the CV curves at varying scan rates ranging from 2 to 100 mV s−1 exhibit a consistent shape, signifying the excellent reversibility of the ASC. The GCD in Fig. 6e were used to evaluate the capacity of devices. The asymmetric supercapacitor (NC)VO/NQGD@CNT//AC has a high specific capacity of 450.6 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. When the current density increases to 10 A g−1, the capacity retention rate remained at 60.9 %, maintaining a specific capacity of 274.4 F g−1, indicating the device's robust rate performance.

Utilizing the capacity obtained from the two-electrode test, the energy density (E, Wh kg−1) and power density (P, W kg−1) of the device can be precisely determined through the application of the respective formulas (10) and (11) [46].
(10)
E =
1 2
C ×
Δ V
2
3.6
(11)
P =
E × 3600
Δ t
where C represents the specific capacity (F g−1), V denotes the potential (V), and t stands for the discharge time (s). The Ragone plot depicted in Fig. 7 a suggests that asymmetric supercapacitor (NC)VO/NQGD@CNT//AC has an ultra-high energy density of 160.2 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 800 W kg−1. This performance outshines many similar materials documented in the literature (Table 1 ). In addition, with such impressive energy density and power density, the device can power a series of 4 white and 4 blue LED bulbs (Fig. 7b). The bulbs remain illuminated even after 100 s, indicating the practical application potential of the device. In Fig. 7c, after 8000 cycles, the (NC)VO/NQGD@CNT//AC remains capacity retention rate of 78.5 %, while the Coulombic efficiency consistently surpasses 98 %, indicating its excellent electrochemical stability and reversibility. The inset graph presents the GCD curves for the initial and final 6 cycles, highlighting minimum time interval change, which further verify the cycling stability of (NC)VO/NQGD@CNT//AC device. The SEM image in Fig. 7d, depicting the sample post-cycling, reveals the presence of agglomeration among the CNTs, and the nanosheets on their surface become smaller. This observation is likely attributed to structural damage incurred by the electrode material during repeated charge–discharge cycles, leading to a degradation in the electrode's performance.

In summary, we have successfully constructed a self-supporting (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8/NGQD@CNT thin film electrode. Using CNT as a self-supporting substrate material, (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8 nanosheets are uniformly wrapped around the outer wall, creating a unique hierarchical nanostructure. The synergistic interactions among multiple metals greatly enhances the charge transfer between ions, contributing to the improved electrochemical performance. The embedded NGQDs effectively increase the number of redox-active sites in a confined space, further accelerating the Faraday redox reaction. This enhancement results in a substantial increase in the specific capacity of the electrode material, achieving a remarkable 3018.2 F g−1. Furthermore, the assembled asymmetric supercapacitor (Ni0.5Co0.5)3V2O8/NGQD@CNT demonstrates exceptional energy density and cycling stability. It demonstrates practical application potential by powering a series of 8 connected light bulbs. These findings underscore the effectiveness of our approach and provide valuable insights for the design and development of advanced energy storage devices. This study introduces innovative strategies for constructing high-performance electrode materials and offers promising directions for future research in energy storage technologies.

Rong Zheng: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Huachen Lin: Software, Investigation. Lin Sun: Validation, Software, Investigation. Yulong Ying: Formal analysis. Bin He: Validation, Software. Yu Liu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22201107, 52203147). Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20220514) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022M711380). Nanjing/Jiangsu XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd of China.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.08.056.

ce:section.ce:para:
